title: C# var and dynamic
content:
### var and dynamic in C#

C# provides two keywords for flexible type declarations: `var` (implicitly typed) and `dynamic` (dynamically typed).

### The var Keyword

`var` tells the compiler to infer the type from the expression. It is strongly typed at compile time.

```csharp
var name = "Alice";        // string
var age = 25;              // int
var price = 19.99;         // double
var items = new List<int>(); // List<int>
```

### Rules for var

| Rule | Explanation |
|------|-------------|
| Must initialize | `var x;` is invalid |
| Cannot be null | `var x = null;` is invalid |
| Cannot change type | Once inferred, type is fixed |
| Local scope only | Cannot be used for fields or return types |

### When to Use var

```csharp
// Good - type is obvious
var dict = new Dictionary<string, List<int>>();

// Bad - type is unclear
var result = ProcessData();  // What type is result?
```

### The dynamic Keyword

`dynamic` bypasses compile-time type checking. Type is resolved at runtime.

```csharp
dynamic value = 10;
Console.WriteLine(value + 5);  // 15

value = "Hello";
Console.WriteLine(value.Length);  // 5

value = new { Name = "Test" };
Console.WriteLine(value.Name);  // "Test"
```

### var vs dynamic

| Aspect | var | dynamic |
|--------|-----|---------|
| Type resolution | Compile time | Runtime |
| IntelliSense | Full support | Limited |
| Performance | No overhead | Reflection overhead |
| Type safety | Strongly typed | Errors at runtime |
| Null assignment | Not allowed | Allowed |

> **Key Point:** Use `var` for obvious types (especially with generics) to reduce verbosity. Use `dynamic` only for interop scenarios or when dealing with dynamic data (COM, JSON, dynamic languages). Prefer `var` over `dynamic` for type safety.

### dynamic Limitations

```csharp
dynamic d = 5;
d.SomeMethod();  // Compiles but throws RuntimeBinderException
```

quiz.question: When is the type of a var variable determined?
Which keyword bypasses compile-time type checking?
